title: Assignment 1
text: My first assignment has four parts.

title: Assignment 1
text:
(a)
I choosed to watch and analyze ‚ÄúVeri Bilimi ve End√ºstri M√ºhendisliƒüi √úzerine Sohbetler - Baykal Hafƒ±zoƒülu & Erdi Da≈üdemir‚Äù
The video basically talks about why the world of OR (Operations Research) has been shifting so much towards AI and Machine Learning lately. The speaker explains this by looking at the value and complexity dimensions of data and breaks it down into four main types of analysis.
For instance, in Descriptive Analysis, the main goal is to strengthen efficiency; that‚Äôs why data mining and visualization tools are at the forefront here. In the Diagnostic part, we‚Äôre looking for the answer to ‚ÄúWhy did it happen?‚Äù and we focus on things like hypothesis testing and clustering to diagnose the current situation.
The most interesting part is Predictive Analytics because everyone is curious about what will happen in the future, so naturally, Machine Learning steps in here. Then there‚Äôs Prescriptive Analysis, which you can think of as ‚Äúwriting a prescription.‚Äù To give the right prescription, optimization and mathematical modeling come into play heavily.
The speaker gives a great supply chain example: tracking daily inventory levels on a chart (Descriptive), then doing a demand forecast (Predictive), and finally performing safety stock optimization based on that data (Prescriptive) actually works like a chain reaction.
It also mentions how hard it is to see this ‚Äúbig picture‚Äù and connect the dots between different classes during university years. That‚Äôs why, as students, it‚Äôs crucial to dive into different areas and gain a more high-level perspective to realize the importance of the education we‚Äôre getting.
Finally, there was a really important emphasis: everything starts with a clear definition of the problem. If you don‚Äôt define the problem correctly, the rest won‚Äôt go well. Also, just building a great model isn‚Äôt enough; how that model reaches the end-user, the defined KPIs, and even the UI (user interface) quality directly impact the success of the project.

title: Assignment 1
text:
(b)
##Open-ended question
Based on the speaker‚Äôs perspective, why is building a mathematically ‚Äúperfect‚Äù model insufficient for the ultimate success of an Operations Research (OR) or AI project? Explain by mentioning the additional factors that influence project success. ##Answer
According to the video, a great model is only one part of the solution. For a project to be truly successful, the following elements must be integrated:
Problem Definition: Everything must start with a clear and correct definition of the problem; otherwise, the model solves the wrong issue.
Implementation and UI: How the model reaches the end-user is critical. The quality of the User Interface (UI) and the ease of use directly impact whether the model is actually adopted.
KPIs and Integration: The success of the model must be measured against defined Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) that align with business goals. Without a bridge between the model‚Äôs output and real-world application, its technical excellence provides little value.
##Multiple-choice question
A supply chain manager monitors daily inventory levels on a dashboard (I), then uses historical data to generate a demand forecast for the next quarter (II), and finally applies an optimization model to determine the ideal safety stock levels to minimize costs (III).
According to the classification mentioned in the video, which of the following correctly identifies these analytical stages? A) I: Diagnostic, II: Predictive, III: Prescriptive B) I: Descriptive, II: Predictive, III: Prescriptive C) I: Descriptive, II: Prescriptive, III: Predictive D) I: Diagnostic, II: Predictive, III: Descriptive ##Answer
B: Monitoring current/past data is Descriptive; forecasting future trends is Predictive; and using mathematical models to determine the best course of action (optimization) is Prescriptive analysis.

title: Assignment 1
text:
Write a Custom Summary Function

data(mtcars) 
head(mtcars) 

                   mpg cyl disp  hp drat    wt  qsec vs am gear carb
Mazda RX4         21.0   6  160 110 3.90 2.620 16.46  0  1    4    4
Mazda RX4 Wag     21.0   6  160 110 3.90 2.875 17.02  0  1    4    4
Datsun 710        22.8   4  108  93 3.85 2.320 18.61  1  1    4    1
Hornet 4 Drive    21.4   6  258 110 3.08 3.215 19.44  1  0    3    1
Hornet Sportabout 18.7   8  360 175 3.15 3.440 17.02  0  0    3    2
Valiant           18.1   6  225 105 2.76 3.460 20.22  1  0    3    1



compute_stats <- function(x) {
  
  result <- list(
    mean = mean(x),
    median = median(x),
    variance = var(x),
    IQR = IQR(x),
    minimum = min(x),
    maximum = max(x)
  )
  
  return(result)
}

title: Assignment 1

numeric_names <- names(mtcars)[numeric_cols]


 for (col in numeric_names) 
{

my_first<-"Seyma"
my_birth_year<-1995
k <- (nchar(my_first) + my_birth_year) %% 15 + 8
print(k)

[1] 13



display_rows <- function(polls_us_election_2016, k) {
  if (k %% 2 == 0) {
    return(head(polls_us_election_2016, k))
  } else {
    return(tail(dpolls_us_election_2016, k))
  }
}


total_na <- sum(is.na(polls_us_election_2016))
total_na

[1] 11604

na_column <- colSums(is.na(polls_us_election_2016))
sort_na <- sort(na_column, decreasing = TRUE)
head(sort_na, 8)

rawpoll_mcmullin adjpoll_mcmullin  rawpoll_johnson  adjpoll_johnson 
            4178             4178             1409             1409 
           grade       samplesize            state        startdate 
             429                1                0                0 



new_data<-polls_us_election_2016
numeric_replacement<-my_birth_year+k
character_replacement<-paste0(my_first, "_", k)
cat(numeric_replacement, "\n")

2008 

cat(character_replacement, "\n\n")

Seyma_13 

print(numeric_replacement)

[1] 2008

print(character_replacement)

[1] "Seyma_13"

for(i in 1:ncol(new_data)) {
  if(is.numeric(new_data[[i]])) {
    new_data[[i]][is.na(new_data[[i]])] <- numeric_replacement
  } else if(is.character(new_data[[i]]) || is.factor(new_data[[i]])) {
    if(is.factor(new_data[[i]])) {
      levels(new_data[[i]]) <- c(levels(new_data[[i]]), character_replacement)
    }
    new_data[[i]][is.na(new_data[[i]])] <- character_replacement
  }
}

if (k %% 2 == 0) {
  head(new_data, k)
  print(head(new_data, k))
} else {
  tail(new_data, k)
  print(tail(new_data, k))
}
